title: Holocarpha obconica
taxonomy: Holocarpha obconica is a species of flowering plant in the daisy family Asteraceae. It is native to California in the United States. The plant is classified as a member of the subfamily Asteroideae within the family Asteraceae. Further classification places Holocarpha obconica in the tribe Madieae. The plant is also known as the San Francisco gumplant or Yerba Buena.
uses: Holocarpha obconica is primarily a plant native to the western United States and Mexico, with limited information available on its medicinal uses. However, its close relative, Holocarpha macradenia, has been used in traditional medicine for its antiseptic and anti-inflammatory properties. In some regions, its leaves or roots might be used to treat minor ailments or injuries, but this is largely anecdotal and not scientifically proven. Further research is needed to determine the full range of potential medicinal uses for Holocarpha obconica. More studies on the plant's chemical composition and biological activity are required to understand its potential health benefits.

intro: Holocarpha obconica, also known as tule weed or tule, is a perennial herb native to the southwestern United States and northern Mexico. The plant has been traditionally used for its medicinal properties, with reported health benefits including anti-inflammatory, antimicrobial, and antioxidant effects, making it useful in treating various ailments such as colds, fever, and skin conditions. The bioactive constituents of Holocarpha obconica include sesquiterpene lactones, flavonoids, and phenolic acids, which are responsible for its medicinal properties. In herbal medicine, Holocarpha obconica is often prepared as a tea, infusion, or tincture, and is sometimes used in combination with other herbs to enhance its therapeutic effects.
